data.title: Zoomd Technologies: From Turnaround To Takeoff
data.description:
Editor's note: Seeking Alpha is proud to welcome The Microcap Lab as a new contributing analyst. You can become one too! Share your best investment idea by submitting your article for review to our editors. Get published, earn money, and unlock exclusive SA Premium access. Click here to find out more »

Investment Thesis

Zoomd Technologies Ltd. (OTCPK:ZMDTF) (TSXV:ZOMD:CA) is a tech-based, mobile-first, cross-channel User-Acquisition Agency that leverages its own suite of proprietary technology to manage User Acquisition (UA) campaigns for brands/agencies globally.

The company did a strategic pivot in 2023 following a changeover in CEO, removing unprofitable business lines, internalizing Account Management for its clients and putting in place a KPI-based business model, to capture more upside on successful campaigns.

This has turned Zoomd into a fast-growing, cash-generating compounder, growing at triple digital YoY growth, generating strong cash flows and significant profitability.

Very importantly, this microcap company is not paid on views or reach like many competitors are, but is paid on getting their customers new, paying clients. That means Zoomd directly influences the bottom line of its clients. This is one of Zoomd key value propositions.

It is competing in the open market segment of advertising, outside of the walled gardens of the tech giants. This fragmented segment represents 48% of advertising spend, and is difficult to navigate for many companies and brands. Managing these campaigns internally has allowed Zoomd to collect expertise in booming markets like fashion/retail advertising, but also in fintech, e-commerce, gaming.

Zoomd has signed huge clients in recent months. The budgets of these global brands like Shein (privately held) and KFC, Ltd. (KFC) are much larger than what Zoomd is currently managing for them, leaving ample room for scaling the business with these existing clients. Zoomd is seeing a lot of momentum from them, as they are expanding in emerging, fragmented, mobile-first markets like Latin America, where Zoomd has been operating for years already.

The stock trades at less than 9x P/E (TTM), less than its closest competitors, despite growing its revenues over 100%+ YoY, being profitable, cashflow positive and having a strong net cash position. Given the current (mispriced) valuation, it wouldn’t take much for the stock to continue its re-rating trajectory, and reach our 12-month price target of $1.6, or ~12x P/E TTM (conservative assumption), and probably sooner than later.

Less than 1% of the float is held by institutions. Besides the fact that a significant 16.57% of shares are held by insiders, most holders are actually retail investors. Buying before hedge funds can get a hold of the stock (and push up the stock price) is the perfect setup for a microcap investor.

Introduction

Zoomd Technologies (ZOMD/ZMDTF) is a MarTech company with a mobile-first approach, which helps brands around the world acquire paying users digitally, more efficiently.

The firm operates outside of the walled gardens of advertising, where tech giants like Meta and Google dominate; this black box where clients can’t control the strategy, and only plan there. This market represents 52% of the advertising budget spend.

It targets the remaining 48%, the millions of websites that companies can market on, and get clients on, but which is fragmented, and where it is very hard to command and control campaigns with no data/expertise. This segment is less tapped into because of that, and ZOMD is here to bridge the gap. ZOMD can function on top of walled gardens, but its value-add is to help its clients navigate UA in the broader 'open internet' of programmatic and long-tail media.

Zoomd Technologies makes its bread-and-butter on User Acquisition (UA) of paying clients, not just outreach like many marketing agencies. Clients know they’ll get a rapid return on their ad spend.

It has built long-lasting business relationships with global brands, and is present in more than 70 countries, with offices in Israel, Canada, Bulgaria, Singapore and Mexico, in just over 10 years of operations.

It also has several strengths: their proprietary media buying optimization technology, large dataset through extensive integration. Zoomd makes its bread-and-butter on the UA of paying clients, and can connect, with no Software Development Kit (SDK), to 600+ media sources), combined with an internalized Account Management and a ROI-based business model (more below).

Zoomd Technologies is a rare find within the microcap world: profitable, cashflow positive, growing at triple digit rates, with large companies in its portfolio on multi-year contracts, it also has expanding margins, and a clean balance sheet with a significant, $12.5 million net cash position and no long-term debt.

But it wasn’t always like this. Up until 2022-2023, it was a break-even, lumpy, average business. Since 2024, Zoomd is now a fast-growing, profitable, cashflow-positive business. Well into 2025, it now looks like a textbook high-ROIC compounder, with a strong operating leverage and overall Product-Market-Fit. In Q1/25, the valuation sits at barely ~8.9x P/E (TTM).

How did that happen?

The Pivot: From Break-Even to Strong Profitability

The turnaround began in 2023 when Ido Almany became CEO. He rapidly decided to exit low-margin or unprofitable business ventures, cutting unnecessary overhead. The company dropped several tech engines that they were trying to incubate as it saw that they didn’t generate profit. It also optimized a lot of the sales processes and customer relations by developing automation tools which allowed Zoomd to interact with clients on a daily basis, giving the business a real boost in profitability and scalability.

Zoomd’s main revenue model was originally recurring SaaS subscriptions in a self-service way, and by taking a share of the budget allocation, leaving a lot of upside from successful UA campaigns on the table. The pivot led the company to put more emphasis on generating revenues from campaign performance, which was facilitated by the internalization of account management. This has allowed Zoomd to be in full control of the budget, and invest where and how much it needs to invest per geography, channels, for maximum UA efficiency, leveraging its in-house expertise.

This new business model allowed the company to grow its revenues and margins (gross margin went from ~38% to 40-43%) much faster, and put its campaign optimization capacities to the test. These changes turned a fragmented, seasonal, lumpy, barely breakeven business into a cash-generative machine. ZOMD has since then posted five quarters of net profitability (since Q1/24), with eight consecutive quarters of operating leverage improvement, showing disciplined execution (more in part 4, below).

Zoomd has been increasing its net cash balance each quarter for the past four quarters, with its positive operational and free cash flow. In this regard, the company has now unlimited runway to achieve its business plan. It is also considering doing a Normal Course Issuer Bid (NCIB) or engaging in M&A activity.

ZOMD’s USP: Differentiated, ROI-driven, Frictionless

Contrary to competitors like Moloco Inc. (privately held), Jampp Inc. (acquired by Affle Ltd. in India), or Liftoff Mobile, Inc. (privately held), Zoomd isn’t trying to sell another ad tech software or Demand-Side Platform (DSP) in a crowded ad-tech-SaaS market. It prefers to act as a growth partner, managing campaigns for its clients using its own tech platform. This removes all frictions of onboarding, which happens when clients need to learn to use a dashboard, hire data scientists or buy media across various channels.

This all-rounded approach, done for the client in a similar way to what agencies would do. However it's also combined with a powerful in-house methodology, which includes proprietary tech and multiple data sources, providing great results (as shown by the many positive client reviews and repeating contracts). That's a sticky value proposition. For companies, externalizing campaign management is particularly interesting. For instance, mid-market marketing teams - those that have some marketing budget but lack significant internal capacities - cannot afford to hire a dedicated team for User Acquisition.

This is what Zoomd describes as one of its secret sauces. By managing the accounts of its clients internally using its own all-in-one platform and owning the entire value chain (from media acquisition to distribution), Zoomd gets the upside if a campaign outperforms. This is what they describe as ROI-driven revenues, which you can’t typically do with a SaaS model where you get paid on the subscriptions only.

That said, Zoomd’s internal already has an expertise in using their technology well, explaining the traction they have had so far. It also provides the platform in a self-service way, for some of the clients (mostly app developers, brands, agencies), that wish to manage campaigns themselves. They benefit from the same tech, which centralizes campaigns across social, search, influencer, programmatic and SDK networks in one interface. It’s just not their core business anymore. For those clients that wish to use the platform themselves, Zoomd’s product does not require the implementation of Software Development Kits (SDKs), making onboarding and deployment even faster.

For better scalability of its services, as part of the automation described above, Zoomd also optimized a lot of the sales processes and customer relations. They are getting new clients at the same rate, but they can really push for larger budget allocations, now that all these automations are in place. The strong ROI that Zoomd has been showing for its clients, is due to the fact that the company originally started as a Search Tech platform in 2012, and was helping publishers improve their on-site queries. This has allowed them to become really good at capturing behavioral signals from the users’ intentions. This early focus on user behavior gave Zoomd a huge data advantage, and allowed the company to develop its current proprietary engine, with smarter audience targeting, predictive modeling on user-intent and cross-channel optimization. Being connected to 600 media sources reinforces the flow of data they receive.

Zoomd’s platform also includes its own DSP, which helps to simplify the buying of programmatic media and to make the process more effective. Their DSP integrates with more than 30 mobile ad/web exchanges, with connections to publishers and also device manufacturers. It runs proprietary algorithms and Albert.ai, a tool they acquired, that increases conversion by adjusting its ad-spend in real time, to where it performs best. Zoomd uses its own data it has gathered over the years, compared to other DSPs that often only rely on third-party data, which allows for more precision in the targeting. On top of this, Zoomd uses influencer and creative services; its influencer network matches influencers with brands in an algorithmic way, completing the large offering umbrella it provides its clients.

Another layer of differentiation against some of its competitors that only cover mobile (i.e. Jampp, LiftOff) or web (traditional agencies), Zoomd is mobile-first but also covers web and Connected TV (CTV) and has a global reach. This is a competitive advantage compared to more local agencies. AppLovin Corporation (APP) used to be mobile-only, but now addresses all of Zoomd’s targeted channels (web, mobile, CTV). But like the competitors mentioned above, it relies on a self-served platform-based model.

This all-in-one platform explains why the Product-Market-Fit of the company is so strong. Zoomd acts as a performant, one-stop-shop. For clients, having all their digital UA activities in one place allows them to pay (and manage) less vendors, meaning lower costs and better visibility into the ROI of their campaigns. Understaffed UA teams can easily outsource entirely to ZOMD, and expect to get instant-ROI on their campaign.

Enterprise Momentum: A New Wave of Big Clients

Zoomd Technologies' momentum is real and increasingly visible. Early this year, it signed contracts with top-notch clients like GoHenry, Acorns, the NBA, Liverpool FC, and Fanatics. These are not just logos, but performance-based contracts that are already signed, with budgets already locked-in for 2025.

When the company signs a contract with a client, it’s usually for a long time; the average duration of the partnership with its top 10 clients is around 3 years, indicating significant commercial retention. This in turn provides ZOMD with visibility and a growing, predictable revenue base, with world-class companies, something most microcaps can only dream of:

Despite still being a microcap, Zoomd is no longer a small, boutique business, it is already gaining traction in over 70 countries around the world, in different geographies, as shown below:

Zoomd Technologies has been gaining market share in Latin America since 2020, as it is very efficient in fragmented (33 countries, where digitalization is more or less mature based on the country), under-penetrated, and mobile-first economies. A similar trend has been happening in Southeast Asia, where Zoomd expanded in 2019. All-in-all, the growing Global Consumer App spend (projected to reach $288 billion by 2030, at a 7.3% CAGR, by Sensor Tower), is a significant tailwind for Zoomd’s mobile-first strategy.

Financial Performance: From Promise to Execution

Zoomd has been executing the last quarters very well, and is proving that its pivot to a capital-light, high-margin model is paying off: they show that the company has operating leverage, increasing its margins faster than it grows its top line. In FY24, the company produced $54.5M of revenue (~70% growth YoY), and grew its gross margins to 39.5%, while managing to drop its operating expenses by over 25%. Without bloating the SG&A costs in the process, this turned a negative bottom line ($4.7M loss) into a profitable one ($8.9M gain). Operating cash flow increased to $7.7M, and CAPEX stayed at just $560K.

It has performed even better in Q1/25, which confirmed the sustainability of the turnaround (in a quarter that has historically weaker, due to seasonality in the adtech business). Yet revenues hit $18.2 million (up +108% YoY), with gross margins climbing to 44.3%, with operating expenses increasing only 10%. Zoomd managed to put $4.8 million (26% net margin, rising by 800% YoY) to the net income line, proving once again their cost discipline and operating leverage. OCF went up to $3.5 million (from $39 thousand the prior year), bringing the net cash position to $12.5 million. All this with no long-term debt, and a clean cap table. Since it pivoted, the ad tech’s operating costs as a percentage of revenues have fallen, from 32% to barely 17% in Q1/25, a clear sign of efficiency.

Since the market saw these numbers, ZMDTF (the US ADR ticker) went up a little less than 2 times ($0.59 on the 21/5/25 to $1.04 on the July 24th 2025).

But is there still meat on the bone?

Valuation: Still Cheap, Despite the Recent Run

Even if the ADR went up ~750% this past year, this profitable microcap barely trades at less than 9x P/E TTM and ~5.85x EV/EBITDA, even with its recent 100%+ top line growth and a great balance sheet (no long-term debt, a nice ~2.6x current ratio, and a strong net cash position).

With a conservative 12x P/E multiple on its TTM revenues (below many MarTech peers), the company would trade at ~$1.6/share, which is approximately 60% higher than current levels, this coming year. The value disconnect is even more evident when annualizing Q1/25 (EPS at $0.05, or $0.2 annualized), suggesting a forward P/E at current prices of only ~5x, meaning the ~60% projected is actually on the lower side. A 12 P/E would mean a stock price for ZMDTF of $2.20, or over 140% higher than it is today.

Any further growth in FY25 would obviously increase the target price. Given current momentum and visibility, and a relatively weak Q2/24. This means next quarter should be a beat, since the CEO claimed that Q2/Q3 are usually the strongest), the stock could rerate very soon, by September.

Looking at all competitors, across all metrics, Zoomd is significantly discounted, despite having a fast growth and high net margin.

More specifically looking at Mobvista, which model is the closest to ZOMD (in the sense that it also provides a hybrid AdTech with Services model, even if the former is more oriented towards gaming/Asia). The latter trades at a ~104x P/E ratio and ~17.3x EV/EBITDA, significantly higher than Zoomd Technologies.

If we assume that revenue will keep growing at a 25% in 2025, going down to 10% in 2028 and stabilizing thereafter, and net margin stabilize at 15%, at a reasonable 12x P/E, then the market cap each year could theoretically be:

One can see that a reasonable upside target would be ~50% within 12-18 months. Once again, this is quite conservative, given the fact that we only have Q1/25 reported for the year, and we already have $13.1 million TTM in net income, leading us to believe that net income could be higher than $10.2 million for the fiscal year.

Risks and Mitigants

Client concentration has long been the key risk of Zoomd. Although we don’t have the precise figures, it is likely that Shein represents probably ~35% of 2024 revenue (but it’s in about 15 countries).

Certainly, one of those geographies is likely the US, and the recent Trump tariffs threats with China scared off many investors, should Shein’s US budgets be cut off with Chinese exports to the US being threatened to that extent. That said, that fear likely peaked in April, during the last market correction that saw the stock retreat meaningfully. But that worst-case scenario now looks less likely, with tariffs stance being much less aggressive. On top of that, they have been working with Shein since 2019, so their business relationship is likely pretty strong at this point.

A cut from Shein’s US budget would hurt the top line but would likely not be business threatening, considering the 15 geographies they’re working on with Zoomd, and the pace at which Zoomd is signing new clients, and the client mix gets more and more balanced:

As one can see above, customer concentration has been less of an issue over time. The company announced that as of March 2025, five of the Company’s customers comprise 85% of its revenues. It’s still high, but as the CEO mentioned, some of those very large clients are global companies that are operating in multiple countries, reducing concentration risk.

On top of that, one could argue that these large contracts also show that a single client can bring meaningful revenues, and demonstrate how profitable those large contracts can be, and an idea of how large the Total Addressable Market could be.

To reassure investors and shareholders, and to support resilient growth, Amit Bohensky (chairman) recently described Zoomd’s diversification approach as a two-dimensional matrix, expanding geographically and in terms of new verticals. As described above, Zoomd retains its top ten clients for an average of over three years, reinforcing our bullish case.

Even if it is way more resilient than before (Operating-Cashflow positive, profitable, large net cash position, commercial momentum), the company is still not immune to macro events; global advertising budgets are cyclical and often go down fast in economic downturns. In certain market segments (fashion, entertainment, gaming), marketing budgets can rapidly be reduced.

That said, Zoomd would likely be more resilient than competitors in a recession, because when ad budgets are reduced, companies reallocate the spending to the most efficient (and measurable) channels, and that’s where Zoomd truly shines. On top of that, the fears related to an imminent recession have eased, with liquidity returning to the system, CPI going down and the business cycle showing resilience. With QE just around the corner, the soft landing is becoming more of a reality, and the coming quarters should remain favorable for businesses overall, in the coming year at least.

Privacy updates taken by Alphabet Inc. (GOOGL) or Meta, or General Data Protection Regulations (GDPR laws), can all be headwinds. But management mentioned in Q1/25 that they will continue to expand to new geographies and industries, and have remained largely unimpacted.

Conclusion: A Likely Rerating in the Coming Months

It’s rare to see a company that still seems cheap after such a re-rate (~750% in the last year), yet this is happening here, as seen in the valuation part of this analysis. It is difficult to anticipate what growth ZOMD will have this year (no guidance has been given), but given the successful pivot to a capital-light, high-margin model, its commercial momentum and a very strong growth in Q1/25, it is fair to believe that it should not disappoint in FY25. Seasonality should be on the investors’ side this time, as the CEO reaffirmed that even if their different verticals tend to over-perform in different quarters limiting revenue lumpiness, Q2 and Q3 of each year tend to be the strongest, which are ZOMD’s next two quarters.

Insiders are aligned with shareholders as they hold 21.91% of shares. Very few institutional investors are on board, and likely not in a big way, yet.

In terms of cash use, it's entirely possible that Zoomd uses its considerable net cash position for M&A activity. The CEO hinted at the possibly to expand their footprint the UK or the US, for example. Considering how they’ve been executing so far, there are reasons to believe a well-made acquisition without (much) dilution could be very fruitful. This potential M&A would be more client-driven to accelerate growth, rather than tech-driven. At the current share price, buying back shares would appear to be a good allocation of capital as well.

Zoomd is very discounted relative to its sector median (28.58 P/E TTM), and could easily gain 50-60% to barely reach 12x P/E, which seems fair value for a qualitative microcap company that still has to prove that it can pursue its growth in the foreseeable future. With $13.1 million of net income (TTM) and $19.2 million (FWD) if Q1/25 is annualized, at a $101 million market cap that brings us at less than 5.5x forward P/E, which given the growth and net margins, appears to be a favorable setup for investors.

data.title: Western Asset Inflation-Linked Opportunities And Income Fund Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Key Takeaways

  • Markets: During the second quarter, increased volatility was marked by geopolitical shocks and macro crosscurrents. The market saw bouts of risk-off sentiment, most notably following the "Liberation Day" tariff announcement on April 2 and the brief U.S., Iran and Israel conflict. Markets subsequently rebounded as most tariffs were delayed, and Iran and Israel agreed to a tentative ceasefire. The front end of the U.S. Treasury (UST) yield curve declined, while long-end yields rose on concerns of rising deficits and potential inflation reacceleration. Throughout the quarter, credit spreads tightened and emerging market (EM) local yields fell. The S&P 500 Index returned 10.57%, and the U.S. dollar weakened versus most EM and developed market (DM) currencies.
  • Contributors: Interest rate positioning and sector exposures contributed to performance.
  • Detractors: The Fund's Treasury Inflation-Protected securities (OTCPK:TIPS) exposure detracted from performance.
  • Outlook: Global growth is expected to slow given heightened unpredictability, but should remain positive. Overall monetary policy remains restrictive and we believe that central banks will continue to cut rates. The Fed remains well positioned to provide support if the U.S. economy falters. While fundamentals remain positive, spreads are at the tight end of historical ranges in some sectors and warrant caution.

Outlook

  • U.S. government policy has caused severe volatility in fixed-income markets over the last several months. Global growth is expected to slow given heightened unpredictability, but should remain positive. U.S. growth is downshifting due to a myriad of factors, including tariff uncertainty, waning benefits from immigration, and reduced government spending in recent years. A significant fiscal boost from European defense and German infrastructure spending should support eurozone growth and provide relief from tariff-related uncertainty. Deflationary pressures in China persist and confidence is weak amid property market concerns, but sentiment is improving with fiscal stimulus and policy easing.
  • Overall monetary policy remains restrictive and we believe that central banks will continue to cut rates. The Fed remains well positioned to provide support if the U.S. economy falters. Public debt levels continue to rise and yield curves may steepen further given concerns over fiscal policies.
  • While fundamentals remain positive, spreads are at the tight end of historical ranges in some sectors and warrant caution. We will continue to look for further periods of volatility to add to spread products.

When the fund engages in transactions that have a leveraging effect on the fund's portfolio, the value of the fund will be more volatile and all other risks will tend to be compounded.

What are the Risks?

All investments involve risks, including possible loss of principal. Fixed income securities involve interest rate, credit, inflation and reinvestment risks, and possible loss of principal. As interest rates rise, the value of fixed income securities falls. Low-rated, high-yield bonds are subject to greater price volatility, illiquidity and possibility of default. Liquidity risk exists when securities or other investments become more difficult to sell, or are unable to be sold, at the price at which they have been valued. International investments are subject to special risks, including currency fluctuations and social, economic and political uncertainties, which could increase volatility. These risks are magnified in emerging markets. Currency management strategies could result in losses to the fund if currencies do not perform as expected. Derivative instruments can be illiquid, may disproportionately increase losses, and have a potentially large impact on performance. Asset-backed, mortgage-backed or mortgage-related securities are subject to prepayment and extension risks. Leverage increases the volatility of investment returns and subjects investments to magnified losses and a decline in value.

data.title: Western Asset Intermediate-Term Municipals Fund Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Key Takeaways

  • Markets: The taxable fixed income market experienced periods of volatility, but posted a modest gain during the second quarter. Investor sentiment was impacted by several factors, including persistent trade uncertainty, signs of global economic strains, questions over future Federal Reserve's ("Fed") monetary policy, mounting fiscal concerns, and hostilities between Israel and Iran. Meanwhile, the U.S. economy experienced its first contraction in three years, while inflation remained higher than the Fed's target. In contrast, the municipal (muni) market modestly declined over the period amid heavy new issuance.
  • Contributors: Overweights to the education and power sectors.
  • Detractors: Duration positioning.
  • Outlook: Muni fundamentals remained resilient. While strong revenue collections are indicative of credit resiliency in the muni market, the pace of credit rating improvement from the three major rating agencies has slowed so far this year.

Performance Review

  • Overweights to the education and power sectors contributed to performance.
  • Overweights to Kentucky and New Hampshire securities were additive for returns.
  • Overweight duration positioning was a detractor from performance.
  • Security selection within the local general obligation ("GO") and special tax sectors were detractors.
  • An overweight to the transportation sector negatively impacted performance
  • An underweight to securities rated AA detracted from results.
  • Underweights to Texas and New York securities were headwind for returns.

Outlook

  • Municipal fundamentals remained resilient. In June, the Census released first quarter 2025 state and local tax collection estimates, which indicated continued revenue growth for state and local governments. Twelve-month trailing collections increased 5% year-over-year to $2.1 trillion, marking a record high level.
  • While strong revenue collections are indicative of credit resiliency in the muni market, the pace of credit rating improvement from the three major rating agencies has slowed so far this year. According to Bloomberg data, total entities upgrades (620) continued to exceed downgrades (472). However, total downgrades by par value of $122 billion outpaced upgrades of $112 billion. Meanwhile, first-time payment defaults totaled $1 billion, exceeding the first half of 2024 level of $711 million.

data.title: Altria Among 6 Dividend Kings To Announce Annual Payout Increases In August
data.description:
This is the latest in my series of articles where I provide predictions of annual dividend increases for long-term dividend growth companies. At the end of June, I provided predictions for 14 dividend growth companies that have historically announced annual payout increases in July. In this article I’ll look at another 15 dividend growth companies that I expect will announce their annual dividend increases in August.

In response to one of the comments on last month’s article, I provided a prediction for heavy automotive equipment manufacturer Cummins, Inc. (CMI). My prediction was for an 8.0% - 10.2% increase. On July 15th, the company announced a 9.9% increase to an annualized $8.00 a share for its 20th year of dividend growth. This latest increase gives the company a forward yield of 2.19%.

In addition, West Pharmaceutical Services (WST) announced its 33rd year of dividend growth with a 4.8% boost to an annualized rate of $0.88. The company usually announces the increase in October, but decided to include it as part of 2nd quarter earnings. This boost gives the company a forward yield of 0.36%.

(All yields are based on stock prices approximately 2 hours before the market open on Thursday, July 31st.)

Results for Dividend Increase Announcements from July

Community Financial System, Inc. (CBU) – 34 years of dividend growth

Prediction: 2.2 – 4.3% increase to $1.88 - $1.92

Actual: 2.2% increase to $1.88

Forward yield: 3.53%

This is the 6th straight year of 4-cent annual dividend growth from the New York State-based regional bank.

The Clorox Company (CLX) – 49 years

Prediction: 1.6 – 2.5% increase to $4.96 - $5.00

Actual: 1.6% increase to $4.96

Forward yield: 3.93%

While earnings are rebounding, Clorox is still in the middle of a business transformation and is holding off on returning to normal levels of dividend growth. This is the consumer goods company’s 2nd year of sub-2% dividend increases.

Cintas Corporation (CTAS) – 42 years

Prediction: 14.1 – 16.0% increase to $1.78 - $1.81

Actual: 15.4% increase to $1.80

Forward yield: 0.81%

The business services company continues to grow its dividend in the mid-teen percentages.

Community Trust Bancorp, Inc. (CTBI) – 46 years

Prediction: 4.3 – 6.4% increase to $1.96 - $2.00

Actual: 12.8% increase to $2.12

Forward yield: 3.92%

This is the largest dividend increase from Kentucky-based regional bank since 2004.

Duke Energy Corporation (DUK) – 22 years

Prediction: 1.9 – 2.9% increase to $4.26 - $4.30

Actual: 1.9% increase to $4.26

Forward yield: 3.55%

The utility’s payout ratio above 70% continues to limit dividend growth to 2 – 3%.

Greene County Bancorp, Inc. (GCBC) – 13 years

Prediction: 11.1 – 16.7% increase to $0.40 - $0.42

Actual: 11.1% increase to $0.40

Forward yield: 1.66%

Earnings growth at the upstate New York bank continues to power double-digit dividend growth.

McKesson Corporation (MCK) – 19 years

Prediction: 11.1 – 14.4% increase to $3.15 - $3.25

Actual: 15.5% increase to $3.28

Forward yield: 0.46%

McKesson continues to grow its dividend by double-digit percentages.

Note: In the previous article, I had the wrong dividend payment for McKesson. I’ve adjusted the dividend amounts above.

Mondelez International, Inc. (MDLZ) – 13 years

Prediction: 9.6 – 11.7% increase to $2.06 - $2.12

Actual: 6.4% increase to $2.00

Forward yield: 3.09%

Dividend growth at the snack and candy company fell from 10% last year to this year’s 6.4% increase due to higher cocoa prices hitting earnings.

Marsh & McLennan Companies, Inc. (MMC) – 17 years

Prediction: 11.7 – 14.1% increase to $3.64 - $3.72

Actual: 11.1% increase to $3.60

Forward yield: 1.80%

The insurance company continues to compound its dividend at 11%.

PPG Industries, Inc. (PPG) – 55 years

Prediction: 3.7 – 5.1% increase to $2.82 - $2.86

Actual: 4.4% increase to $2.84

Forward yield: 2.70%

Dividend growth continues to slow at the paint and coatings company in response to slowing earnings.

Republic Services, Inc. (RSG) – 23 years

Prediction: 6.0 – 7.8% increase to $2.46 - $2.50

Actual: 7.8% increase to $2.50

Forward yield: 1.08%

Waste company Republic Services hit the high end of my expectations with an 8% boost.

The J. M. Smucker Company (SJM) – 29 years

Prediction: 1.9 – 2.8% increase to $4.40 - $4.44

Actual: 1.9% increase to $4.40

Forward yield: 4.03%

The food company continues to focus on paying down debt rather than on fast dividend growth.

Stanley Black & Decker, Inc. (SWK) – 59 years

Prediction: 0.6 – 1.8% increase to $3.30 - $3.34

Actual: 1.2% increase to $x3.32

Forward yield: 4.93%

This is the 2nd year of 1% dividend growth as the tool company continues to restructure its operations.

Skyworks Solutions, Inc. (SWKS) – 11 years

Prediction: 1.4 – 2.9% increase to $2.84 - $2.88

Actual: Deferred to August

The semiconductor developer should announce its next dividend increase when it reports earnings on August 5th.

Predictions for Dividend Increases in August

There are 15 long-term dividend growth companies I expect to announce their annual increases in August. First, here is my prediction for one featured company:

Altria Group, Inc. (MO) – 55 years

With a dividend yield around 7% and a growth history of more than half a century, making it a Dividend King, Altria is an income investor’s dream. The tobacco company has three business segments, including two reportable segments: Smokeable Products and Oral Tobacco Products. The Smokeable Products segment covers Altria’s cigarette and cigar business, under multiple brands including Marlboro cigarettes and Black & Mild cigars. The Oral Tobacco Products segment includes sales of Copenhagen, Skoal, on! and Red Seal tobacco dip and pouches. The third segment is “All Other”, which includes joint ventures and investments such as the NJOY brand of e-vapor cigarettes, the company’s investments in Anheuser-Busch InBev and Cronos, and the company’s research and development.

As smoking and tobacco use have fallen in the United States, sales have stayed flat or fallen slightly. The sales drop for the Smokeable Products and Oral Tobacco segments ranges from a 1% drop in sales volume for the combined oral tobacco products to a 10% drop in cigarettes. Recognizing this, Altria has shifted its business mission to transitioning smokers to what the company calls less harmful choices, encapsulated in the motto “Moving Beyond Smoking”. Over the last several years, the company has invested in alternatives to traditional tobacco products. For example, in 2022 Altria entered into a joint venture with a subsidiary of Japan Tobacco to market heated tobacco stick products in the U. S. and in 2023 Altria finalized its acquisition of e-vapor company NJOY Holdings.

The company’s done an impressive job continuing to grow earnings in what is an admittedly hostile environment for tobacco use. EPS growth in 2024 was 3.4%, which the company followed up with a 6% growth in the first half of 2025. The company also continues to buy back shares – after repurchasing more than 9% of its outstanding shares since 2020, Altria recently authorized an additional $1 billion share buyback program. There are some headwinds that the company is facing – after divesting itself of its investment in JUUL, the U. S. International Trade Commission issued an import ban on NJOY vaping devices and pods in March due to a patent infringement ruling in favor of Juul Labs. However, the company’s Oral Tobacco segment represents about 10% of total company sales and less than 15% of company income so, although sales of NJOY products are on a downward trajectory, this should have little impact on Altria as a whole. With EPS growth, Altria will be able to extend its dividend growth record to 56 years with a mid-single digit boost.

Prediction: 3.9 – 5.9% increase to $4.24 - $4.32

Predicted Forward Yield: 6.98 – 7.11%

Here are my predictions for 14 other long-term dividend growth companies which should announce annual increases in August:

Company # Yrs Industry Prediction (%) New Annual Rate
American States Water (AWR) 71 Utilities - Regulated Water 8.5% - 10.6% $2.02 - $2.06
The California-based utility has the distinction of having the longest dividend growth streak among publicly traded companies. The company provides both water and electrical utility services, with the size of its water business being far larger than the electricity business. Business is good, with 2024 EPS growth of 11% and first quarter 2025 EPS growth of 13% far surpassing the company’s long term dividend growth of 8 – 9%. With the good EPS growth, I expect this year’s dividend growth to be on the high side of that range, with the chance of something slightly larger. Predicted Forward Yield: 2.75 – 2.80%
BancFirst Corporation (BANF) 32 Financial - Regional Banks 6.5% - 7.6% $1.96 - $1.98
BancFirst Corporation is the bank holding company for three separate bank chains: BancFirst Bank in Oklahoma, and Pegasus Bank and Worthington Bank in Texas; as well as independent insurance agency BancFirst Insurance Services. The company’s sales have shot up over the last several years, but over the last year operating expenses grew just as fast, resulting in 2024 EPS growth of less than 2%. 2025 is shaping up to be a better year, with 11% EPS growth in the first quarter of the year. With a payout ratio below 30%, the company can maintain its 7% dividend growth rate despite the lack of earnings growth. Predicted Forward Yield: 1.57 – 1.58%
Badger Meter, Inc. (BMI) 33 Technology - Scientific and Technical Instruments 25.0% - 29.4% $1.70 - $1.76
Designer and manufacturer of water management products Badger Meter had a very good 2024, and it looks like 2025 is going to be good also. The company grew sales 18% and EPS 35% in 2024, and is following up with another 31% EPS growth in the first quarter of 2025. This small-cap company is debt free and hasn’t been repurchasing its shares, meaning that the additional income is entirely available for dividend growth. Badger’s situation was similar last year and the company came through with a 26% dividend boost. I’m expecting another increase at the same rate or even close to 30% this year. Predicted Forward Yield: 0.89 – 0.92%
Broadridge Financial Solutions (BR) 18 Technology - Information Technology Services 10.2% - 11.4% $3.88 - $3.92
The financial technology leader routinely grows its dividend at 10%+ year-over-year, powered by double-digit earnings growth. Adjusted EPS grew by 10.3% in 2024 and is expected to grow another 8 – 12% in 2025, with the company posting 18% growth in the first three quarters of the fiscal year. The company doesn’t spend its free cash flow on share buybacks so the company can focus on growing the business and boosting its payout, meaning that investors can look forward to another double-digit boost. Predicted Forward Yield: 1.57 – 1.59%
CBOE Global Markets, Inc. (CBOE) 15 Financial - Financial Data & Stock Markets 12.7% - 15.9% $2.84 - $2.92
CBOE runs one of the world’s largest derivatives and securities exchange network. Originating from the Chicago Board Options Exchange, the company has seen dramatic volume growth in stock options, which has been a major part of its earnings growth. Adjusted EPS growth came in at 10% in 2024, with an additional 16% growth in the first quarter of 2025. Investors can expect dividend growth to be around the same level as last year’s 14.5% boost. Predicted Forward Yield: 1.18 – 1.21%
Chemed Corporation (CHE) 17 Healthcare - Medical Care Facilities 7.0% - 9.0% $2.14 - $2.18
Chemed has two primary business lines: it operates the VITAS Healthcare Corporation, which owns facilities that provide end-of-life care and it owns and operates the Roto-Rooter drain cleaning provider. About 2/3rds of the company’s revenues and earnings come from VITAS. VITAS acquired the health care facilities of Covenant Health in April 2024, helping to boost earnings by 10% in 2024. However, the company recently reduced its 2025 guidance due to revenue limitations from the cap on Medicare reimbursements. With the uncertainty, investors can expect a slowdown from last year’s 15% dividend boost to something closer to the company’s 8% long-term growth rate. Predicted Forward Yield: 0.51 – 0.52%
Carlisle Companies Incorporated (CSL) 49 Industrials - Building Products & Equipment 17.0% - 19.0% $4.68 - $4.76
Once an industrial conglomerate, Carlisle has transformed itself into a building products company, focusing on weatherproofing and construction materials. The company has divested all its non-building products businesses and acquired some new companies that fit into the plan. It’s used excess cash flow to buy back more than 18% of its shares since 2020 and built a long-term dividend growth rate in the mid-teen percentages. It looks like investors can expect the dividend growth to continue – despite flat revenues and a small drop in adjusted EPS in the first half of 2025, business in 2024 was outstanding with revenue growth of 9% and adjusted EPS growth of 30%. With a payout ratio around 20%, Carlisle will be able to match last year’s high teen percentage payout boost this year. Predicted Forward Yield: 1.25 – 1.27%
Dover Corporation (DOV) 70 Industrials - Specialty Industrial Machinery 1.0% - 1.9% $2.08 - $2.10
This industrial conglomerate is known for its lengthy dividend growth record, second only to American States Water Company among publicly traded companies. Dover’s dividend growth has slowed over the last decade, with the company boosting its annual dividend by only 2 cents over each of the last 5 years. That may be changing. Dover’s gone on a purchasing spree, acquiring 8 companies over the last 4 years. This is starting to show up in earnings, with 2024 adjusted EPS growth of 4% and expectations of 12% in 2025. But until the expectations are realized, dividend growth rates should stay flat. I expect another 2-cent increase, with the possibility of a 4-cent increase. Predicted Forward Yield: 1.14 – 1.15%
Federal Realty Investment Trust (FRT) 58 Real Estate - Retail REIT 0.5% - 1.4% $4.42 - $4.46
The retail REIT has been going through some tough times. Over each of the last 5 years, the company has grown its dividend by only 4 cents, and growth in funds from operations (FFO, a measure of REIT profitability that accounts for the tax benefits of real estate ownership) has been in the 3 – 4% range recently, not enough for faster dividend growth. FFO growth was 3.4% in 2024 and 3.7% in the first quarter of 2025. With a growing share count (up 12% since 2020), investors can expect another year of slow dividend growth. Predicted Forward Yield: 4.72 – 4.77%
Ingredion Incorporated (INGR) 15 Consumer Defensive - Packaged Foods 3.8% - 5.0% $3.32 - $3.36
Ingredion manufactures and markets food additives like coloring and sweeteners to food and beverage companies. While the new presidential administration has made one of its lines of efforts the reduction in artificial additives, business at Ingredion is booming, with the company posting 13% adjusted EPS growth in 2024 and expectations of additional growth of up to 9% in 2025. While the company has an active share buyback program – having repurchased 4% of its shares since 2020 – I think the company will accelerate its dividend growth from last year’s 2.6% boost. Look for an increase in the mid-single digit range. Predicted Forward Yield: 2.51 – 2.54%
Intuit Inc. (INTU) 14 Technology - Software 20.2% - 23.1% $5.00 - $5.12
Known by most people for their Turbo Tax personal tax filing software, Intuit has multiple software products that are all going gangbusters – Credit Karma, QuickBooks and Mailchimp are all seeing double-digit growth in both sales and profits. Intuit grew its 2024 EPS by 24% to $10.43 and raised its 2025 EPS growth guidance throughout the year to 26%. The company’s established a good long term growth rate of close to 20% over the last decade and with last year’s and this year’s EPS growth, investors can expect an increase around that amount, if not higher. Predicted Forward Yield: 0.62 – 0.63%
MGE Energy, Inc. (MGEE) 50 Utilities - Regulated Electric 2.2% - 4.4% $1.84 - $1.88
Earnings posted by the Wisconsin-based gas and electric utility move essentially with energy usage and is dramatically affected by the weather in the mid-western United States. 2024 EPS grew by only 2.5% as warmer-than-normal weather reduced gas demand. However, as weather in the first quarter of 2025 cooled down relative to the same period in 2024, EPS jumped more than 20%. MGE Energy has a fairly consistent dividend growth rate of 5%, but last year’s EPS growth means that this year’s dividend boost will be smaller. Predicted Forward Yield: 2.20 – 2.25%
Nordson Corporation (NDSN) 62 Industrials - Specialty Industrial Machinery 10.9% - 12.8% $3.46 - $3.52
The industrial conglomerate has consistently put up dividend growth in the teens, sporting a compounded growth rate of 14% over the last decade. But while Nordson has been able to grow sales in the mid-single digit percentages, increased interest expense is impacting the bottom line, with adjusted EPS down 1% in 2024 and 1.5% in the first half of 2025. Nordson continues to generate good free cash flow, but with the slowdown in earnings, I expect that this year’s dividend increase will be a little below the average. Predicted Forward Yield: 1.60 – 1.63%
Westlake Chemical Corporation (WLK) 21 Basic Materials - Specialty Chemicals 6.7% - 8.6% $2.24 - $2.28
Westlake has two primary business units – Performance and Essential Materials, which operates its polyethylene and vinyl chemical production and Housing and Infrastructure Products, which manufactures products for building materials. While the company posted EPS of $4.64, up 25% from the previous year, it’s a far cry from the EPS of $17.35 the company posted in 2022. While the company is turning the corner, it won’t be able to build on its 15% compounded growth rate over the last 5 years. Instead, I expect a slight increase from last year’s 5% boost into the high single digits. Predicted Forward Yield: 2.81 – 2.86%

Summary

It was a mixed bag for dividend growth investors in July. We saw five companies reward investors with 10%+ increases, led by medical distributor McKesson and business services company Cintas with a 15% boost. As always, there were companies that are either going through business transformations or just simply suffering from a business slowdown that grow their dividends by small amounts to keep their dividend growth streak going. Included in this category are consumer products company Clorox, tool company Stanley Black & Decker, food manufacturer J. M. Smucker, and utility Duke Energy, each of which announced sub-2% increases.

Coming up in August, we have several Dividend Kings that will announce their annual increases, including two companies with records of dividend growth for seven decades. American States Water and Dover Corporation, having grown their payouts for 71 and 70 years respectively, have the two longest dividend growth streaks among publicly traded companies. There are four other companies with 50+ year records of payout growth, putting them in the “Dividend King” category: Federal Realty Corporation, Altria, Nordson Corporation, and MGE Energy.

I have high expectations for dividend growth in August. We should see double-digit percentage growth from 6 or 7 companies, including CBOE, Carlisle Companies, Intuit, Broadridge, Nordson, Badger Meter, and possibly American States Water. In contrast, we’ll see 1 – 2% increases from Dover Coporation (yet another year of small growth from them) and Federal Realty. data.title: Core PCE Inflation Rises 2.8% In June, Higher Than Expected
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

The Fed’s preferred inflation gauge, the core PCE price index, rose 2.8% year-over-year in June. This was higher than the expected 2.7% growth and a slight pickup from May. On a monthly basis, the core index was up 0.3%, as expected.

The headline index was up 2.6% year-over-year. This was just above the expected 2.5% annual growth and up from 2.4% in May. On a monthly basis, the index was up 0.3%, as expected..

PCE Price Index

Personal consumption expenditures (PCE) measures and tracks changes for all domestic personal consumption. Core PCE measures the changes in personal consumption less food and energy, making it less volatile than the headline PCE. The PCE Price Index is calculated using PCE data and is a key way to measure changes in purchasing trends and inflation.

The adjacent thumbnail gives us a close-up of the trend in YoY core PCE since January 2012. The first string of red data points highlights the 12 consecutive months when core PCE hovered in a narrow range around its interim low. The second string highlights the lower range from late 2014 through 2015. Core PCE shifted higher in 2016 with a decline in 2017, 2019, and 2020, with a major jump in 2022.

The next chart below shows the monthly year-over-year change in the personal consumption expenditures (PCE) price index since 2000. Also included is an overlay of the core PCE (less food and energy) price index, which is the Fed's preferred indicator for gauging inflation. The 2% benchmark is the Fed's conventional target for core inflation.

For a long-term perspective, here are the same two metrics spanning five decades.

Inflation: Fed's 2% Target

The Fed traditionally uses the PCE Price Index as their preferred inflation gauge; however, another common measure of inflation is the Consumer Price Index.

In their latest meeting, the Fed held rates steady at 4.25-4.50%, as expected. This marked the fifth straight meeting the Fed has left rates unchanged. The statement from the meeting revealed the Committee believes inflation remains "somewhat elevated" but that they are strongly committed to returning inflation to its 2% objective.

data.title: Franklin Small Cap Growth Fund Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Key Takeaways

  • Markets: US stocks advanced during the second quarter of 2025. After rebounding from April's lows, equity markets continued to rally through June despite bouts of volatility. The S&P 500 ((SP500), (SPX)) Index and Nasdaq (NDAQ) Composite Index closed the period with solid gains after hitting new record highs at the end of June, while the Dow (DOW) Jones Industrial Average hovered near its all-time high. Temporary delays in tariff hikes, reduced fears of a recession, growing expectations of interest-rate cuts later in the year and easing geopolitical tensions helped drive US stocks higher. Large-capitalization equities gained the most, followed by mid- and small-cap stocks, and growth stocks outperformed value by a wide margin in all three market-cap tiers.

  • Detractors: Stock selection effects were the primary cause of the fund's underperformance of the Russell 2000 Growth Index benchmark. Stock selection in the information technology (IT) sector was a leading relative detractor. Stock selection and an overweight in the consumer staples sector and stock selection in the energy sector also hindered relative performance.

  • Contributors: The bulk of the fund's relative strength came from stock selection and an underweight in the health care sector. To a lesser degree, the fund benefited from stock selection in the financials and consumer discretionary sectors. Overall, sector allocation decisions worked in our favor.

  • Outlook: We are hopeful as we enter the second half of the year that financial markets can focus on the potential long-term structural benefits of a Trump 2.0 policy agenda that might include initiatives to rebalance global trade and stimulate growth through tax reform and deregulation.

Performance Review

  • The fund's return was positive but lagged that of the Russell 2000 Growth Index. Relative returns in the IT sector were negatively impacted by stock selection in the semiconductors and semiconductor equipment industry, where Onto Innovation (ONTO) was a leading detractor. The process control solutions company issued disappointing second-quarter earnings guidance that weighed on its shares. We believe Onto operates in an attractive space (process control, metrology, inspection, lithography) during a time when semiconductor packaging complexity is increasing dramatically.

  • Nutrition products company BellRing Brands (BRBR) was a top detractor in the consumer staples sector. While the company reported strong performance across its protein-focused portfolio, it raised concerns about inventory reductions and profit-margin pressures in the second half of the year. We believe BellRing continues to benefit from various tailwinds, including new capacity, improved inventory tracking and ramped up promotions and marketing.

  • In contrast, in the industrials sector, BWX Technologies (BWXT) was a leading individual contributor to relative returns. The company is a supplier of nuclear components and fuel to the US Navy and the commercial nuclear power industry. We believe BWX can profit from the government's focus on bolstering defense spending.

Outlook

  • Our outlook for financial markets remains cautious, reflecting the magnitude and complexity of the current political and economic climate. While challenging, active management allows us to pursue alpha by taking advantage of market volatility as a chance to initiate or build up positions in what we believe are high-quality businesses. These businesses have strong balance sheets and market-leading competitive positions, and they are levered to durable secular growth themes.

  • One theme is technology, which continues to be a large area of opportunity as more companies adopt artificial intelligence in their efforts to lower costs and increase productivity. We see significant growth potential for the industrials sector, fueled by trends that include the reshoring of US manufacturing, electrification and meaningful infrastructure investment. Our outlook for health care is also bullish. Wide-ranging innovations (genomics, robotics, personalized medicine) and meaningful demographic shifts support our convictions.

  • Deregulation could positively impact the energy sector by increasing fossil fuel production and streamlining the permitting process. Deregulation and lower taxes could serve many companies in the financials sector as they could help boost profits. With a stronger economy and lower regulatory burden, we think banks may see an increase in lending activity, and fintech innovation could surge.

  • Our strategy is designed to address both upward and downward market cycles, and a diversified approach helps us participate in both ongoing market strength and anticipated rotation in market leadership. We invest in what we regard as great businesses positioned to potentially benefit from secular growth over a long-term horizon.

data.title: Franklin U.S. Government Ladder 1-5 Year SMA Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Key Takeaways

  • Markets: There were large swings in financial market sentiment across the second quarter of 2025. During April, fixed income spreads generally widened amid US President Donald Trump's "Liberation Day" tariff announcements due to fears that they could lead to a drop in growth and a new round of inflationary pressures. A subsequent 90-day pause for many of the increased levies helped calm market nerves. The passage of the "One Big Beautiful Bill" spending package after quarter-end further helped reduce fixed income market uncertainties. Over the period, US Treasury (UST) yield changes were mixed. Very short- and intermediate-dated yields witnessed declines, while longer-maturity UST yields increased. Yields declined by 16 basis points (BPS) to 3.72% on the two-year UST note (US2Y) and by 15 bps to 3.80% on the five-year UST note (US5Y).
  • Contributors: There were no material contributors to returns.
  • Detractors: Exposure to the two to five year portion of the UST yield curve.
  • Outlook: The US Federal Reserve's (Fed's) reaction function is complicated. Fears of increased inflation due to fiscal policy are likely to keep the Fed on hold, in our assessment. We are looking for at most one rate cut for the remainder of the year.

Performance Review

  • Yield curve positioning hurt the strategy's results due to an overweight to duration exposure in the two to five year portion of the curve as UST yields rose.

Outlook

  • In our view, the Fed is unlikely to cut interest rates soon. Despite recent signs of cooling inflation, Fed officials remain cautious due to ongoing tariff-related uncertainties. Companies are expected to raise prices as pre-tariff inventories diminish, leading to a likely rise in core goods inflation. Furthermore, average US tariff rates remain historically high despite a temporary easing in US-China trade tensions. We therefore expect potentially higher inflation and slower growth. With inflation still above target and risks skewed to the upside, we expect the Fed to maintain its pause. Amid still-resilient retail sales, we believe the Fed is likely to stay on hold until there is clear evidence of labor market deterioration without a simultaneous rise in inflation.
  • The "One Big Beautiful Bill" spending package will further increase the US deficit over the next decade. Combined with factors such as uncertain trade policy (which has raised stagflationary concerns) and an expansionary fiscal policy (which implies greater debt issuance), we expect short-term rates to remain elevated due to the pause in Fed rate cuts and longer-term rates to continue to climb higher through the rest of the year.

A subsequent 90-day pause for many of the increased levies helped calm market nerves. The passage of the "One Big Beautiful Bill" spending package after quarter-end further helped reduce fixed income market uncertainties. Over the period, US Treasury yield changes were mixed. Very short- and intermediate-dated yields witnessed declines, while longer-maturity UST yields increased. Yields declined by 16 basis points to 3.72% on the two-year UST note (US2Y) and by 15 bps to 3.80% on the five-year UST note (US5Y). Ten-year UST note (US10Y) yields moved two bps higher to 4.23%.
  • Contributors: Overweight exposure to USTs with five years to maturity.
  • Detractors: Exposure to the two to 10 year portions of the UST yield curve.
  • Outlook: The US Federal Reserve's (Fed's) reaction function is complicated. Fears of increased inflation due to fiscal policy are likely to keep the Fed on hold, in our assessment. We are looking for at most one rate cut for the remainder of the year.

Performance Review

  • Yield curve positioning hurt the strategy's results due to an underweight to duration exposure in the two portion of the curve as UST yields fell at the front end of the curve. In contrast, overweight exposure to the five-year area of the UST curve contributed to returns.

Outlook

  • In our view, the Fed is unlikely to cut interest rates soon. Despite recent signs of cooling inflation, Fed officials remain cautious due to ongoing tariff-related uncertainties. Companies are expected to raise prices as pre-tariff inventories diminish, leading to a likely rise in core goods inflation. Furthermore, average US tariff rates remain historically high despite a temporary easing in US-China trade tensions. We therefore expect potentially higher inflation and slower growth. With inflation still above target and risks skewed to the upside, we expect the Fed to maintain its pause. Amid still-resilient retail sales, we believe the Fed is likely to stay on hold until there is clear evidence of labor market deterioration without a simultaneous rise in inflation.
  • The "One Big Beautiful Bill" spending package will further increase the US deficit over the next decade. Combined with factors such as uncertain trade policy (which has raised stagflationary concerns) and an expansionary fiscal policy (which implies greater debt issuance), we expect short-term rates to remain elevated due to the pause in Fed rate cuts and longerterm rates to continue to climb higher through the rest of the year.

Glossary
